title: Public Records Request — Chicago Generative AI Records

communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

FOIA Officer
City of Chicago
121 N. LaSalle Street
Chicago, IL 60602

Dear FOIA Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City of Chicago's use of generative AI tools by city employees.

Pursuant to the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140/3, we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City of Chicago employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City of Chicago employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The City of Chicago has published a Roadmap for AI through its ChiTech portal, laying out recommendations to assist in the planning of the city's AI strategy, including maximizing public benefit and helping city employees focus on their core missions (see City of Chicago, "Roadmap for AI," https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/sites/chitech/home/roadmap-for-AI.html). The roadmap acknowledges that city employees can quickly access generative AI tools via web browser or smartphone, and that many employees have already begun using generative AI for work purposes. It recommends enabling employees by providing access to preferred platforms and identifying generative AI thought leaders within city government (see City of Chicago, "Recommended Actions," https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/sites/chitech/home/roadmap-for-AI/recommended-actions.html). A 2025 report noted that cities across the U.S., including Chicago, have been embracing AI guidelines for local government workers amid growing concerns about unmanaged "shadow AI" use (see Route Fifty, "Cities across the US are embracing AI guidelines for local government workers," Aug. 2025, https://www.route-fifty.com/artificial-intelligence/2025/08/cities-across-us-are-embracing-ai-guidelines-local-government-workers/407561/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, administrators can access interaction logs through the Google Workspace Admin Console under Reporting > Audit and Investigation. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Illinois's Freedom of Information Act explicitly enumerates "electronic data processing records, electronic communications, and recorded information of any kind" as public records. 5 ILCS 140/2(c). The Public Access Counselor within the AG's office has issued binding opinions confirming that electronic messages are public records. Governor Pritzker's Executive Order 2024-07 directed the creation of an AI governance framework, and the Department of Innovation and Technology (DoIT) is developing AI use policies including documentation requirements. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

title: Public Records Request — Austin Generative AI Records

Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Information Officer
City of Austin
P.O. Box 1088
Austin, TX 78767

Dear Public Information Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City of Austin's use of generative AI tools by city employees, including Archistar AI and Pano AI.

Pursuant to the Texas Public Information Act, Tex. Gov't Code § 552.221, we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City of Austin employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City of Austin employee use of Archistar AI, Pano AI, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

In August 2024, the Austin City Council approved a three-year, $3.5 million contract with Archistar, an AI-powered automated building permit assessment platform that reads submitted drawings and evaluates designs against digitized planning and building codes (see KUT Radio, "Austin will use AI to evaluate residential construction plans," Oct. 11, 2024, https://www.kut.org/housing/2024-10-11/austin-tx-artificial-intelligence-building-applications-permits-construction; see also Cities Today, "Austin launches AI-driven building permit software," https://cities-today.com/austin-launches-ai-driven-building-permit-software/). The City has also deployed Pano AI, a wildfire detection system using 26 cameras with 360-degree AI-powered technology to monitor 437 square miles across Austin and surrounding counties, sending real-time fire information to first responders (see KVUE, "New AI system helps Central Texas first responders detect wildfires as they happen," https://www.kvue.com/article/news/local/new-wildfire-detection-system-texas-austin-energy-pano-ai/269-6c9ade64-ab41-47a0-8407-cd053f4da3e1).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Archistar's eCheck platform generates compliance reports from submitted permit applications that may be exported. Pano AI maintains detection logs and alert histories accessible through its administrative dashboard. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. The Texas Public Information Act defines "public information" as information collected, assembled, or maintained in connection with official business by a governmental body, including electronic data. Tex. Gov't Code § 552.002. The Texas Supreme Court confirmed in City of Dallas v. Abbott that text messages and electronic communications are public information. The Department of Information Resources issued Generative AI Guidelines (2024) recommending that agencies document AI tool usage and treat AI-generated outputs as public information. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

title: Public Records Request — PortlandTriMet Generative AI Records

communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
TriMet (Tri-County Metropolitan Transportation District of Oregon)
1800 SW First Avenue, Suite 300
Portland, OR 97201

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to TriMet's use of generative AI tools by agency employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by TriMet employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

TriMet CIO Ethan Benatan serves on the GovAI Coalition Board, a network of over 300 government agencies committed to responsible AI use (see https://www.sanjoseca.gov/your-government/departments-offices/information-technology/ai-reviews-algorithm-register/govai-coalition). The City of Portland's Digital Services team has developed a generative AI chatbot prototype using Google Dialogflow to help residents schedule permit appointments, reflecting the region's engagement with generative AI tools in government operations (see Portland.gov, "One conversation at a time: Digital Services presents AI chatbot to public service professionals," July 2, 2025, https://www.portland.gov/digital-services/news/2025/7/2/one-conversation-time-digital-services-presents-ai-chatbot-public). Oregon's Department of Administrative Services has also released statewide guidance on responsible use of generative AI by state employees (see https://apps.oregon.gov/oregon-newsroom/OR/DAS/Posts/Post/CIO-announces-training-to-state-employees-on-responsible-use-of-generative-AI).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini or Dialogflow-based tools, logs may be available through Google Cloud administrative consoles. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under Oregon's Public Records Law, ORS 192.329(2)-(5), every person has a right to inspect any public record of a public body. "Public record" includes "any writing that contains information relating to the conduct of the public's business, including but not limited to court records, mortgages, and deed records, prepared, owned, used or retained by a public body regardless of physical form or characteristics." ORS 192.311(5)(a). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by TriMet employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. TriMet's participation in the GovAI Coalition through CIO leadership further underscores the agency's engagement with AI tools in its official operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

Dear Data Practices Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

The St. Paul Police Department has implemented Axon Auto-Transcribe for use by homicide, robbery, and sex crimes investigators to transcribe interviews with victims, witnesses, and suspects (see GovTech, "Police in St. Paul, Minn., Implement First AI Use Policy," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/police-in-st-paul-minn-implement-first-ai-use-policy). The department adopted its first generative AI policy in October 2025, which permits Axon Auto-Transcribe for drafting police narratives while prohibiting officers from using publicly available tools like ChatGPT for report writing (see St. Paul Police Policy 236.06, https://www.stpaul.gov/sites/default/files/2025-10/236.06%20Generative%20Artificial%20Intelligence%20(GenAI)%2010.17.25_1.pdf). Officers are required to review and verify AI-generated transcripts for accuracy before including them in reports. Minnesota police departments more broadly are increasingly using AI tools to assist with report writing (see MPR News, "An AI tool is generating police reports for some Minnesota departments, prompting legal concerns," Oct. 8, 2025, https://www.mprnews.org/story/2025/10/08/minnesota-police-departments-using-ai-to-generate-reports).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Axon Auto-Transcribe logs and transcripts may be available through the Axon Evidence platform's administrative tools. ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act, Minn. Stat. 13.03, all government data collected, created, received, maintained, or disseminated by a state agency, political subdivision, or statewide system is presumed public unless classified otherwise by statute. Minn. Stat. 13.01, 13.03. AI chatbot interaction logs and AI-generated transcripts created or used by city employees in the course of their official duties qualify as government data under this definition. The St. Paul Police Department's published Generative AI policy (Policy 236.06) confirms that Axon Auto-Transcribe is an approved tool used in official police operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. See "Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA," ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025), https://www.executivegov.com/articles/army-garciga-application-owners-ai-records-mgmt.
